period: c. 814 BCE – 146 BCE (Punic); 44 BCE – 698 CE (Roman and late antique)
title: Carthage
summary: The Phoenician trading city that became Rome's most dangerous rival and was completely destroyed — the paradigm case of the losers' civilization in Western historical memory, known almost entirely through the accounts of those who destroyed it.
hook: We know almost everything about Carthage from its enemies. Rome destroyed it so thoroughly that it survived only in Roman memory — a civilization we understand primarily through the eyes of those who found it so threatening that they erased it from the earth. What is lost when the winners write the only history?

beginner.thePlace:
Stand on the headland near Tunis and you see white villas, a beach, and the sea. Little there tells you that one of the richest cities of the ancient world once filled this ground. Carthage was a giant. Its harbors held hundreds of warships. Its merchants ruled the sea trade of the western Mediterranean. For more than a century it was the rival that truly frightened Rome.

Then Rome wiped it out. The strangest fact about Carthage is the one we have to start with. Its books and records are gone. Rome destroyed them so completely that we are mostly left with what Rome chose to tell us.

Carthage left us very little in its own words. So when we picture Carthage, we are mostly looking at it through Roman eyes. The winners told the story. The losers fell silent. That silence shapes every page that follows.
beginner.physicalWorld:
Carthage sat on a peninsula in what is now northern Tunisia. This was the narrowest crossing of the Mediterranean, where Africa and the island of Sicily almost touch. Whoever held this spot controlled the sea lanes. Ships from Spain, Sardinia, Sicily, and North Africa all passed close by.

The military harbor was a wonder. It held 220 warships in covered berths set around a round basin. A small island in the center let the admiral watch every ship at once. Triple walls ringed the city. Even the Romans who came to tear them down were impressed.

Little of that stands there now. The peninsula is a suburb of Tunis. Roman ruins lie under modern houses, and the older Punic city lies deeper still, broken into fragments. Diggers have worked these layers for decades. Carthage gives up its pieces slowly, one buried wall at a time.
beginner.theLayers:
Carthage did not die just once. It died, was rebuilt, and died again.

The first Carthage was the Punic city, founded by Phoenician traders. This was the trading empire and the rival of Rome. Rome destroyed it in 146 BCE.

Then Rome rebuilt it. Roman Carthage grew into one of the great cities of the empire. It shipped grain across the sea and buzzed with ideas. A young man named Augustine studied there before he became one of the most important Christian thinkers in the West. Two famous early churchmen lived and died there too.

Then new conquerors came: the Vandals, then the Byzantines. In 698 CE Arab armies took the peninsula, and Carthage faded as a major city for good. Three different civilizations had lived on this same ground. The earliest one barely left a voice we can still hear.
beginner.whatItBecame:
Carthage became a classic example of an erased civilization.

The picture that survives is grim. Carthage gets remembered as cruel, dishonest, and brutal, a place that sacrificed its own children. But that picture is almost entirely Roman. The Romans had a phrase, Punica fides, meaning "Punic faith." They used it as an insult to mean a liar who breaks his word.

Is any of it true? We honestly cannot be sure. Carthage cannot answer back. Its archives are ashes. Its own historians wrote things we no longer have. We cannot read how Carthaginians saw themselves, their gods, or the wars they fought.

Rome destroyed the city with armies. It destroyed the city's good name with words. Both jobs were done thoroughly, and only one of them can ever be undone.
beginner.whoClaimsIt:
Now Tunisia claims Carthage with pride. It is a link to a past older than the Arabs and older than the Romans. The hero of that past is the general Hannibal.

Hannibal earned his fame. In 218 BCE he marched an army from Spain, over the Alps, and into Italy. He crossed icy mountain passes in winter, losing thousands of men and most of his war elephants. Then he spent fifteen years fighting inside Italy itself. At the battle of Cannae in 216 BCE, he surrounded and destroyed a Roman army of about 70,000 men. Soldiers still study that battle now.

Yet he never captured Rome. No fresh troops were sent to help him. Rome's allies stayed loyal. In the end he was called home, beaten at Zama in 202 BCE, and later forced to flee. Forty-six years after that, Rome destroyed his city.

One of the most dangerous enemies Rome ever faced came from a place Rome then rubbed off the map.
beginner.theLongLife:
"Carthage must be destroyed." In Latin, Delenda est Carthago. An old Roman senator named Cato reportedly ended every speech with that line, no matter the real subject. The words became a saying for total obsession.

There is something strange in that. Carthage was already doomed, yet the hatred for it needed a name and got one. The phrase became a kind of monument. Carthage won a weird sort of fame through the fury of its enemy.

Now the buried city is being found again. Diggers work the old Carthage site near Tunis, and other Punic places like Motya in Sicily and Kerkouane in Tunisia. They uncover houses, harbors, workshops, and temples. Sometimes this evidence backs up the Roman story. Sometimes it quietly disagrees.

The true voice of Carthage is still mostly lost. But the city itself is slowly rising back into the light.

general.thePlace:
Walk the seaside suburbs of Tunis and you would never guess that one of antiquity's great powers lies beneath your feet. Carthage commanded the narrowest point of the Mediterranean and grew rich taxing the ships that had to pass it. For more than a century it was the one state that genuinely menaced Rome. Then Rome obliterated it.

That is the fact every account of Carthage must reckon with first. Almost everything we know about this civilization comes from the people who destroyed it. The libraries of Carthage, said to hold its literature, history, and records, were handed to North African kings after 146 BCE and then lost. What survives of Punic writing is thin: farming manuals, dedications, scraps on tombstones. There is no Punic history and no Punic philosophy in any extended form.

This is the heart of the problem. To reconstruct Carthage, we must read Roman and Greek accounts while never forgetting that their authors had reasons, military and political, to distort what they described. Archaeology helps and sometimes corrects them. But the city's own voice is nearly gone.
general.physicalWorld:
One of the most argued-over sites in Carthaginian archaeology is the tophet, a sacred enclosure where urns holding the cremated remains of infants were buried. Excavators have recovered thousands of these urns, some marked with inscriptions to the gods Baal Hammon and Tanit. Greek and Roman writers read this as proof of child sacrifice, a society burning its babies as offerings.

Some recent scholars read it differently. On this view the tophet was a special burial ground for children who died young, and the urns reflect the grim rate of natural infant death rather than ritual killing. The dispute matters far beyond one cemetery, because it decides whether the Roman image of Carthage as a place of monstrous worship rests on anything real or is simply propaganda.

The evidence can support either reading, and that ambiguity is typical of Carthage. The city left enough to spark the question and not enough to settle it. We are left weighing buried jars against the words of people who wanted Carthage condemned.
general.theLayers:
There is a sharp irony in what rose from Carthage's ashes. The Roman city built on the ruins became one of the most intellectually fertile places in the late ancient world. Tertullian, among the earliest major theologians to write in Latin, came from Roman Carthage. Cyprian, who shaped the early church's thinking about the authority of bishops, led the church there and was executed there. Augustine of Hippo, later one of the most influential thinkers in Western Christianity, studied rhetoric in Carthage before his conversion.

So the city Rome had wiped out was rebuilt under Roman rule and became a cradle of Christian thought that echoed for over a thousand years. Meanwhile the Punic city's own intellectual life, whatever it amounted to, vanished completely. The afterlife of the place is real and well documented. The inner life of the civilization that founded it is the part history cannot return to us.
general.whatItBecame:
Punica fides, "Punic faith," was the Roman shorthand for treachery and bad faith. It packaged the Roman view of Carthaginian character into two words, and it shows how durably the victors' version of the defeated can outlive the war itself.

The phrase did real work. It excused Rome's own deceptions during the Punic Wars by assigning Carthage a deep moral rot. A people who cannot be trusted, the logic ran, do not deserve the courtesies owed to honorable enemies, so their destruction was less a conquest than a cleansing. The story made annihilation feel justified.

This idea outlived Rome and seeped into later Western thinking, including the language of empire in modern centuries. The notion that North African or Semitic peoples are somehow untrustworthy helped feed later Western stereotypes, drawing in part on Roman propaganda about Carthage. Pulling the real Carthage free of this inherited slander is slow work, and its main lesson so far is how completely a hostile tradition can flood the empty space left by a destroyed archive.
general.whoClaimsIt:
Hannibal's Italian campaign, fought from 218 to 203 BCE, is studied in military academies not because he won but because of what he managed while losing. Leading an army with cavalry and war elephants across the Alps in winter remains one of the boldest logistical feats in military history, though scholars still argue over which passes he actually used. At Cannae in 216 BCE he encircled and destroyed a Roman force of roughly 70,000 men, a maneuver of tactical geometry that commanders have tried to copy ever since.

And yet Hannibal never took Rome. No reinforcements reached him from Carthage, a failure of support he himself blamed for his defeat. Rome's web of Italian alliances held firm despite his victories. He was recalled to Africa, beaten at Zama in 202 BCE by Scipio Africanus, and eventually fled Rome's long reach before his death. Rome destroyed his city forty-six years later. Modern Tunisia, for its part, has embraced him as a national hero, claiming a heritage older than both Rome and Islam.
general.theLongLife:
The phrase Delenda est Carthago, "Carthage must be destroyed," was attributed to Cato the Elder, who reportedly tacked it onto every Senate speech regardless of the subject at hand. It gave Carthage a strange afterlife. The ruin it demanded became proverbial for the thoroughness of a victory, while the obsession behind it became proverbial for single-minded fixation. Carthage won a measure of immortality through the sheer intensity of Rome's hatred.

Meanwhile, archaeology is slowly assembling a fuller picture to set beside the Roman texts. Excavations at Kerkouane, a Punic town in Tunisia that survived because Rome never rebuilt over it, reveal daily life, domestic architecture, and craft production with no Roman overlay. Motya in Sicily documents the Punic presence across the western Mediterranean, and divers have traced the shape of Carthage's old harbor underwater.

The voice of Carthage remains nearly silent. The city, though, is being recovered piece by piece from the ground that buried it.

advanced.thePlace: The methodological problem of Carthage is also an ethical problem: the complete destruction of Carthage was a deliberate act of cultural erasure, and the historical tradition that preserved Carthage's memory was produced by those who performed that erasure. This creates a specific kind of epistemic injustice: the Carthaginians cannot speak for themselves in the historical record, and what speaks in their place is the representation their destroyers chose to preserve. Archaeological evidence provides the most reliable supplement to the textual record, but it cannot reconstruct the subjective experience of Carthaginian civilization.
advanced.physicalWorld: The military harbor of Carthage — the cothon described by Appian — is one of the most remarkable pieces of ancient military infrastructure in the Mediterranean world. The circular harbor, with covered berths for 220 warships arranged around a central admiral's island, was an integrated naval base that could simultaneously shelter, repair, and deploy a substantial fleet. Its design reflects Carthage's identity as a maritime trading and military power: the city's wealth came from controlling Mediterranean sea lanes, and its military power rested on naval dominance rather than land armies.
advanced.theLayers: The tophet debate — whether the urns of infant remains represent child sacrifice or an infant burial ground — is one of the most contested issues in ancient history. The Roman and Biblical sources are unambiguous: Carthaginians sacrificed children, burning them as offerings to Baal Hammon. The archaeological evidence is ambiguous: the urns contain infant remains with dedicatory inscriptions, but the cause of death cannot be determined from cremated remains. Lawrence Stager's work defended the child sacrifice interpretation; Josephine Quinn and others have more recently argued for the infant cemetery interpretation. The debate is unlikely to be fully resolved because the evidence is genuinely ambiguous.
advanced.whatItBecame: The strategic problem that Hannibal could not solve — how to turn tactical military victories into strategic political outcomes — is one of the most analyzed problems in military history. Hannibal defeated Rome in virtually every significant battle of the Second Punic War, yet Rome won the war. The explanation involves Rome's reserve manpower, the alliance system that held despite Cannae, Carthage's failure to reinforce Hannibal, and Scipio Africanus's strategy of carrying the war to Africa rather than trying to defeat Hannibal in Italy. The analysis of why brilliant tactical victories failed to produce strategic success is one of the founding problems of military strategy studies.
advanced.whoClaimsIt: The destruction of Carthage in 146 BCE was not simply a military victory; it was a deliberate act of permanent elimination. The Roman Senate voted to destroy the city, sell its population into slavery, and prohibit future habitation on the site — an act without parallel in Roman practice against other defeated enemies. Whether this reflected Carthage's continued commercial wealth and potential military revival, accumulated Roman fear from three wars, Cato's obsessive advocacy, or simply opportunity — the decision used the full destructive power of Roman military victory in a way that had no precedent.
advanced.theLongLife: The phrase 'delenda est Carthago' has entered Western political discourse as a figure for single-minded political obsession. Its contemporary uses carry the weight of its historical origin: a civilization so thoroughly destroyed that its erasure became proverbial. Recovering Carthage from within this memory — attending to what the archaeological record shows rather than what the Roman texts claim — is an ongoing scholarly project that has made substantial progress but remains incomplete.

foundation.theStory: Carthage is the paradigm case of the lost civilization — a sophisticated, wealthy, powerful civilization known almost entirely through the accounts of those who destroyed it. Understanding Carthage means confronting the epistemological problem at the center of ancient history: how do we know civilizations whose own voices have been silenced? It also means engaging with the ethics of historical destruction — what it means to eliminate not just a city but the record of a civilization's self-understanding.
foundation.theDebate: The central historical debate concerns how much of what we know about Carthage is reliable versus how much is Roman propaganda. The specific debate about child sacrifice — did Carthaginians sacrifice children, or were the tophet burials something else? — is the sharpest version of this general question. The broader debate asks how we should use archaeological evidence to supplement and correct hostile textual sources.
foundation.whyItMatters: Carthage matters for understanding how historical memory works — how the winners' account of the losers shapes our understanding of the past. It matters for understanding Mediterranean history — Carthage's destruction made Roman Mediterranean dominance possible. And it matters for military history — Hannibal's campaigns raise fundamental questions about the relationship between tactical and strategic success.
questions: Almost everything we know about Carthage comes from Roman sources — and Rome had strong reasons to portray Carthage negatively. How should historians use hostile sources? Is it possible to recover a more balanced account, or does the hostile perspective inevitably distort everything it touches?

Hannibal defeated Rome in virtually every major battle of the Second Punic War but ultimately lost the war. What does this suggest about the relationship between tactical military success and strategic political outcomes?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Carthage scholarship has been transformed in the past fifty years by archaeological excavation and critical methods applied to Roman textual sources. The foundational archaeological work at Carthage was done by a UNESCO international campaign in the 1970s-80s. The revisionist textual scholarship is represented by Serge Lancel's Carthage (1995) and by Josephine Quinn's In Search of the Phoenicians (2018), which challenges the entire concept of 'Phoenician' as an ethnic category.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidentiary situation for Carthage is uniquely challenging. The textual evidence is almost entirely hostile (Roman and Greek) or fragmentary (Punic inscriptions, agricultural texts). The archaeological evidence has been partially compromised by Roman rebuilding and inadequate early excavations. The tophet evidence is genuinely ambiguous on the specific question of child sacrifice. What survives allows partial reconstruction of material culture and some religious practices, but cannot recover the intellectual and literary life destroyed with Carthage's archives.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Carthage must be explicit about what kind of claim it is making and what evidence supports it. Arguments based primarily on Roman textual sources must acknowledge those sources' systematic bias. Arguments based on archaeological evidence must engage with genuine ambiguity — particularly on the tophet. The best recent scholarship combines critical source analysis with archaeological evidence while remaining appropriately humble about the limits of what can be recovered.
questions: Compare the evidentiary situation for Carthage (known almost entirely through hostile sources) with the evidentiary situation for the Han dynasty (known primarily through Chinese official sources). What methodological strategies would you apply in each case to mitigate the bias?

The tophet controversy cannot be resolved from available evidence alone. What principle should guide historical judgment in cases of genuine evidential ambiguity? Should the moral stakes of the conclusion affect how ambiguous evidence is interpreted?

research.topic: Hannibal's military campaigns and their significance
research.content: The military history of Hannibal's campaigns in the Second Punic War (218-201 BCE) — including the Alpine crossing, the battles of Trebia, Lake Trasimene, and Cannae, and the defeat at Zama — is well-documented from multiple ancient sources and has been extensively analyzed by military historians. The tactical innovations at Cannae (double envelopment) are particularly well-established and continue to be studied in military education.

research.topic: Punic material culture and urban organization
research.content: Archaeological work at Punic sites outside Carthage — particularly at Kerkouane in Tunisia (an unromanized Punic town), Motya in Sicily, and Punic communities in Spain and Sardinia — has substantially expanded knowledge of Punic material culture beyond the Roman textual record. This work is gradually constructing a picture of Punic civilization that supplements and sometimes corrects the Roman account.

How Carthaginians understood their own civilization — their history, religion, political philosophy, literary and intellectual tradition — cannot be recovered. The destruction of Carthage's archives eliminated whatever written record existed; only fragmentary inscriptions and an agricultural treatise survive. What can be responsibly inferred: Carthage was a literate society with administrative, commercial, and religious text production; it had intellectual traditions that influenced the Mediterranean world. What must remain unknowable: the content of Punic historical, philosophical, or literary writing; how Carthaginians understood the Punic Wars; how they understood their own religion and its critics.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Treacherous Enemy
comparativeNarrative.content:
The Romans kept Carthage alive in memory as the enemy that had to be destroyed. Their historians — Livy on the Punic Wars, Polybius on Hannibal's campaigns, Appian on the Third Punic War — all built the same picture: Carthage was dangerous in war, treacherous in peace, and morally rotten at its core. The Roman phrase Punica fides, meaning "Punic faith" but used as an insult for oath-breaking, summed up the verdict.

These accounts were not neutral reports. They were written to explain why Rome nearly lost, to justify the three wars that followed, and to make the final destruction of Carthage in 146 BCE look like an act of reason rather than a choice to wipe a civilization off the map. Every passage about Carthage's cruelty or dishonesty was doing political work. Reading Roman sources on Carthage means reading them as arguments, not as evidence about what Carthage was actually like.

comparativeNarrative.name: Carthage as National Heritage
comparativeNarrative.content:
Tunisia today claims Carthage as a founding chapter in its national story. This reading places Carthage before Rome and before the Arab conquest — a way of saying that the land had a great civilization long before either. Hannibal appears on Tunisian currency. He is treated as a national hero, a man who took the fight to Rome on Roman soil for fifteen years and nearly won.

This is a powerful story, but it has a complicated edge. The connection between ancient Carthage and modern Tunisians is not ethnic or cultural in any direct sense. Centuries of Roman, Arab, and Ottoman presence lie between them. What modern Tunisia is claiming is territorial heritage — the idea that a great civilization lived here first — not a direct line of descent. That distinction matters for how honestly the claim can be made.

comparativeNarrative.name: Hannibal as Strategic Failure Despite Tactical Brilliance
comparativeNarrative.content:
Military writers have puzzled over Hannibal for centuries because his career raises a question that has no easy answer. He won at Trebia, Lake Trasimene, and Cannae. He defeated Rome in battle after battle. He spent fifteen years fighting inside Italy itself. And he still lost the war.

The standard explanations are well known. Carthage never sent him enough reinforcements. Rome's Italian allies stayed loyal despite his victories. Rome had the manpower to absorb enormous losses and keep fighting. Without siege equipment, Hannibal could not take walled cities. When the Roman general Scipio Africanus carried the war to Africa instead of trying to beat Hannibal in Italy, Hannibal was recalled and defeated at Zama. Military writers from Napoleon to Liddell Hart have used his campaigns to argue that winning battles is not the same as winning wars — that turning a victory on the field into a lasting political outcome requires much more than tactical skill.

rabbitHole.label: Ancient Egypt
rabbitHole.reason: Both are ancient Mediterranean civilizations whose historical legacy is shaped by the surviving sources — contrasting Carthage (known through hostile sources) with Egypt (known through its own abundant records) illuminates how the nature of surviving evidence shapes historical understanding

rabbitHole.label: The Roman Empire
rabbitHole.reason: Rome's destruction of Carthage was the decisive event in Roman conquest of the western Mediterranean — understanding what Carthage was and why Rome destroyed it is prerequisite to understanding Roman imperial expansion

rabbitHole.label: Navigation
rabbitHole.reason: Carthage's power was fundamentally maritime — its commercial empire and military capacity both rested on naval supremacy; the Carthaginian tradition of maritime exploration is one of the earliest records of systematic exploration

rabbitHole.label: Alexandria
rabbitHole.reason: Both were great ancient cities whose libraries were destroyed — comparing what survived (Alexandria's legacy) with what did not (Carthage's) illuminates how intellectual heritage is preserved or lost through historical catastrophe

rabbitHole.label: The Silk Road
rabbitHole.reason: Carthage controlled the western Mediterranean's primary trade routes as the Silk Road connected East Asia to the Mediterranean — comparing the two commercial systems illuminates different models of trade-based power

reference.author: Serge Lancel
reference.title: Carthage: A History
reference.annotation: The most comprehensive scholarly synthesis of Carthaginian history — combines archaeological and textual evidence to produce the fullest available account of Punic civilization. Lancel is consistently critical of the Roman sources and attentive to what the archaeological evidence does and does not support.

reference.author: Josephine Quinn
reference.title: In Search of the Phoenicians
reference.annotation: Quinn's revisionist account challenges the entire concept of 'Phoenician' as an ethnic category and argues for understanding Punic communities on their own terms rather than through Greek and Roman ethnographic categories.

reference.author: Adrian Goldsworthy
reference.title: The Fall of Carthage: The Punic Wars 265-146 BC
reference.annotation: The best accessible narrative history of the Punic Wars — Goldsworthy's military-historical approach is rigorous and his account of Hannibal's campaigns is the most readable in English.

reference.author: Richard Miles
reference.title: Carthage Must Be Destroyed
reference.annotation: The best popular history of Carthage — Miles combines archaeological and textual evidence accessibly and is appropriately critical of Roman propaganda.

reference.author: Polybius
reference.title: The Histories
reference.annotation: The earliest and most detailed surviving narrative of the Punic Wars and Hannibal's campaigns; the entry's account of the Alpine crossing, Cannae, and the Second Punic War rests substantially on Polybius.

reference.author: Livy
reference.title: Ab Urbe Condita (The History of Rome)
reference.annotation: The fullest extant Roman narrative of the Punic Wars; the entry treats it as a principal—and openly hostile—textual source for Carthage, to be read 'as arguments, not as evidence.'

reference.author: Appian
reference.title: Roman History (Punica)
reference.annotation: The principal surviving source for the Third Punic War and the destruction of 146 BCE; the entry draws on Appian's description of the cothon for its account of Carthage's military harbor.

reference.author: Lawrence E. Stager
reference.title: Child Sacrifice at Carthage: Religious Rite or Population Control?
reference.annotation: Stager's excavation-based defense of the child-sacrifice interpretation of the tophet, which the entry names as one pole of the central scholarly debate over Carthaginian religion.

commerce.title: The Fall of Carthage
commerce.author: Adrian Goldsworthy
commerce.note: Start with the Punic Wars narrative — the most accessible entry point into what Carthage was and why its conflict with Rome mattered

commerce.title: Carthage Must Be Destroyed
commerce.author: Richard Miles
commerce.note: The best popular history — Miles combines archaeological and textual evidence accessibly and is appropriately critical of Roman propaganda

commerce.title: Salammbô — Gustave Flaubert (1862)
commerce.author: Gustave Flaubert
commerce.note: Historically unreliable but the most vivid literary reconstruction of Punic Carthage — essential as a reception history document

popularCulture.title: Delenda est Carthago
popularCulture.description: Cato the Elder's saying — "Carthage must be destroyed" — gets tacked onto every political speech he gave, no matter the topic. That part is accurate. Today people use the phrase to mean any obsessive, single-minded demand. It captures the relentless hatred well. It leaves out what that hatred cost: a whole civilization was wiped off the map, and almost every word we have about it was written by its enemies.

popularCulture.title: The Battle of Cannae and double envelopment
popularCulture.description: Hannibal's move at Cannae in 216 BCE — letting Roman troops push forward, then wrapping around them from both sides — is still taught at military schools today. The tactic is real and was brilliant. What gets left out is the bigger picture: Hannibal won almost every battle and still lost the war. The "Cannae solution" can make a single victory look like the whole story.

popularCulture.title: Salammbô — Gustave Flaubert (1862)
popularCulture.description: Flaubert's novel is set in Carthage during a war between the city and its unpaid mercenaries after the First Punic War. The writing is vivid and dramatic. It gets the broad time and place roughly right. It distorts badly: the religion, the violence, and the women are all shown through a 19th-century European eye that treats Carthage as strange and exotic rather than as a real, complex society.
